item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact: I expanded 2207 in part because it was drawing unfavorable compsrisons to this SCP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that's a twist ending. I thought it was going to be like 507, I thought it was going to be like 2207, and instead it's something new and equally creepy. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, you're right. I'll fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1, but i have one qualm near the end.

Reality Description: SCP-1985 did not travel to another reality, but immediately reverted to its altered form without any teleportation occurring.
Retrieval: SCP-1985 reverted to its base form after six months of study without any teleportation occurring.

you cant revert into something new, even if they dont know which form came first it would still be something like "scp entered its altered state/form"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't particularly like it, but the twist at the end sold it for me. Just barely, though. Weak upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And also, do you intend to do something with that information (another SCP, a tale, etc.)?

Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Big and bold and brave, but for me it all works and nicely creates a sense of doom and intrigue. Potentially could be linked to SCP-2003?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so: I've loved this SCP ever since I first found it, and had been looking for it to upvote it ever since I joined the site, since it's one of those that stuck with me. That being said, and after reading it again, I agree with some of the other posters who say it's a bit cluttered. The idea comes through for me, however, so it's not that big of a deal. Besides, I'm afraid I don't have a reasonable solution to suggest, so I might as well just be complaining of fullness.

Just out of curiosity, though: Do you, as the author, know what the current K-Class scenario mentioned in the last addendum is? And also, do you intend to do something with that information (another SCP, a tale, etc.)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I see this as "507, except we don't have to worry about him dying." I like the subtle, 'not worth mentioning' comment that she's a lesbian.

Would love more testing logs, but with SCPs with this much potential, I usually do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm among those that feel this is too similar to 507, though my objection isn't the obvious references - they're well done and feel natural - but to the most basic concept. Hopping to other realities (though this time, the Foundation can influence/control their nature!) just isn't interesting enough, to me, to need much variation.

093 does the apocalyptic alternate reality better, 507 does the reality hopping more interestingly.

That said, this could make an amazing series of tales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm aware of those and also even more I either didn't use or haven't used yet. I omitted GH & GK because they give the impression (to me) that they're from different classification systems. (The - partial - one I present is supposed to be one of several different versions.) I might put them in eventually though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a few things to say about this one.
A document recovered from the Marinas Trench (a story with like +211 rating) mentions a GH-0 Dead Greenhouse scenario. Dunno if that's worth a mention. Also, SCP 540 uses GK-class hostile greenhouse scenario.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the down-side, the experiment log seems like "Visions Of A Better World" but in list form and less interesting.

I happen to agree with you; unfortunately, I had to put so much work into the main SCP article in a short-ish time period that I left the experiment log a little… minimalist. I like the ideas I put in there but currently it's not quite where I want it. Which is why I'll be coming back to it & expanding it. (Though I do hope that what's currently there is upvote-worthy, of course.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
3 collapsibles and 2 external supporting pages, which in turn have a combined 6 footnotes and links to 8 other mainlist items; I can think of only three other articles that are this intricate and they are open-ended collaboration pieces.

I can feel the world-building effort coming off this in waves, and I for one appreciate it. Short and sweet, 173 style articles have their place; but it's the sweeping, in-depth, setting-establishing pieces like this that contribute most to the long term success of this wiki. +3, one for each page.

On the down-side, the experiment log seems like "Visions Of A Better World" but in list form and less interesting.

item.title: Re: Mega-Post Warning
item.content:encoded: There've been a number of articles recently that do cross-linking well. I just don't think this is one of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This….. it just… and then… by the….

Son of a…

­+ + to the fucking badass 1!

Your formatting accidentally made it Level 1 Header text. I feex.- Scantron

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: BTW, I took another pass dedicated to making this easier to read, with the help of the inestimable TroyL (huge thanks be unto him). So if readability was an issue for you before, give it another look-see and lemme know if there are still problems. Thanks!

item.title: Mega-Post Warning
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking of that recent article that listed a bunch of classifications, and people were all "ew, stahp".

Actually, the idea of that was extremely popular, but people didn't like the execution, and the attempt to make a single canon that didn't take into account what was already being used. The latter two issues are covered here by specifying one of many extant classification systems,

This reads like a laundry list thereof.

The list at the end of the article is a laundry list, but it's intended to convey the sheer number of extended trips she's been sent on. I am willing to potentially rephrase that, but at the moment I'm not sure it is necessary.

If you think the article can't stand on its own quality-wise, and/or it doesn't interest you because of the 507 similarity, that's fine and perfectly valid.

I'm just objecting to voting based on dislike of any crosslinking or dislike of any multiple use of K-class scenarios, or based on the fact that 507 is 'canon' to this article and that it's directly based on 507 & other SCPs in-universe, or stuff like that which is unrelated to article quality.

You're of course free to vote based on any of those things, and in fact I expected to get some downvotes based on those things. But I feel it's part of my job as staff to discourage that trend. It goes along with Clef's proposal back in summer: this site needs meta-story. Crosslinking and connected articles and articles that share a story with another article are what draw people into the site. Those things are, in fact, the reason I joined, and the reason many other people joined as well.

…And of course in this case this case butters my own bread, since it's my article in contention, so there's that motivation too. ;)

(Also, a trivial nitpick: this article isn't actually based on 507. The log being close to 507's was a change made after the fact, and something that I might revert because I'm not sure how much it adds. Though of course the link to 507 is now pretty core, so I'm not changing that. It's based on the thought experiment of "how could any SCP end the world?", my ideas of how the SCP universe works (specifically the kicker reveal is based on that) and … a certain older project which you can probably figure out by reading the supplemental "Recovered Information" page.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking of that recent article that listed a bunch of classifications, and people were all "ew, stahp". This reads like a laundry list thereof. That and the linking to 507 feel too much like trying to shove together some canon that doesn't really need to be shoved.

I'd say derivative of 507 is a good descriptor. In reading this, I was thinking to myself that it was just a 507 that could be controlled. The explicit link to 507 itself only reinforces this. This article can't stand on its own, and if it tried to, my reaction would just be, "Well, we've already got 507."

What works are, as I said, the suggestion that we're undergoing a K-class event in this universe, as well as the idea of a Foundation from elsewhere sending us a project of theirs. I'm not sure if those could be reworked into another article, but as this one stands, I don't feel that those two items are enough to float it past "it's just another 507".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the implications of this SCP, that she came from our world before a restructuring even and that we might currently be undergoing another event. I love alternate reality stuff, especially if the alterations exist in our own dimension/timeline.
Aside from that, I think it's unique and interesting on its own. I haven't read the supplemental documents yet, but I'm still going to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. You have a lot of supporting materials, but none of it is necessary to understanding and appreciating the SCP for what it is. Very cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I'd like a little more background on 1985 herself. Foundation Agent? Researcher? D-Class? We're giving her plenty of concessions, makes me want to know why she's so trusted. (Omagerd, it's actually o5-3! J/K!!)

None of the above. Ordinary human being, former civilian (was dying of that cancer, actually). I might add a little bit of that in, though not to the main article, which is already long enough.

[EDIT: lol, I eventually retconned this to former Foundation researcher because it adds more 'oomph'. rest applies.]

The reason she's so trusted even as a Euclid is for many reasons, but primarily for these two: (1) she can get away with murder in alternate realities (including finding out all kinds of information that might otherwise be restricted), (2) she has an opportunity to breach containment about every month. (She could probably get that tracking device removed in an alternate reality, couldn't she?)

So the "Class 1 privileges" (which still require her to be accompanied by personnel whenever possible except in her containment chamber) are in large part to keep her under control by making her complicit in her own containment.

(Frankly, that's how I see most 'free-roaming' SCPs, minus some that are just silly. A little bit of controlled freedom can be powerful.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Makes the wheels spin a bit thinking it through. Makes me wonder if someone didn't shove 507 and a pair of headphones through 914.

Personally, I'd like a little more background on 1985 herself. Foundation Agent? Researcher? D-Class? We're giving her plenty of concessions, makes me want to know why she's so trusted. (Omagerd, it's actually o5-3! J/K!!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see this got put up. I was worried it wouldn't make it through the edit-eckoning. Good job dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know Moose isn't trying to coattail 507, it's just the differences between the two isn't enough just yet.

Any suggestions on differentiation? (Also, does the changed teleportation make a difference, or the stuff in the new supplemental page?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds nifty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand that, but the similarities of the two simply overshadows the differences for me to enjoy it. It's not bad, it's just too similar. I know Moose isn't trying to coattail 507, it's just the differences between the two isn't enough just yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Teleportation changed. She can take personnel with her, but when she goes, the 'safety' bits of 507's teleportation don't function correctly, and she just appears somewhere near the surface of the Earth's crust. She can usually survive the fall, dig out of the ground, or swim to the surface of the ocean (but not always, and then she has to be sent off again). But personnel coming with her can't predict where she'll end up and therefore risk dying.

Thoughts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, I appreciate the feedback.

Downvoted for not only being a 507 the Foundation can conveniently control, but trying to justify its own existence in crosslinking to 507. (Note: 507 is one of the first SCP articles I ever read, and continues to be a favorite.)

Plus a lot of expungements. Granted, I straight up haven't recently seen an article (that was not written horribly) that had a lot of expungements, so maybe I'm just unused to them.

Can you elaborate? Please note that crosslinking to something is not a bad thing. A lot of expungement, when done properly, is also not a bad thing. (For one, I can tell you that I certainly know everything behind the expungements.)

If you feel it's not interesting enough to stand on its own and feels too derivative of 507, or if you can point out why in this case the expungement is bad, that's another thing.

(BTW, I'm moving some of the material to a sub-page and expunging a little less in between typing this.)

Plus this and the log seem to just be trying to use as many of those new *K classifications as possible in one place.

Again, can you elaborate? Is it simply that it's not interesting besides that? Because using a bunch of K classifications is not a bad thing.

(Also please note I came up with exactly zero of those classifications. They are all taken from other articles.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for not only being a 507 the Foundation can conveniently control, but trying to justify its own existence in crosslinking to 507. (Note: 507 is one of the first SCP articles I ever read, and continues to be a favorite.)

Plus why do we give her the internet. Sure we can kill her at any time, but that doesn't seem necessary.

Plus this and the log seem to just be trying to use as many of those new *K classifications as possible in one place.

Plus a lot of expungements. Granted, I straight up haven't recently seen an article (that was not written horribly) that had a lot of expungements, so maybe I'm just unused to them.

The one thing that really caught my attention, and I'm glad you point it out in the article, is the whole thing of "We're undergoing a K-class event right now and we have no idea what it is." But for just that payoff, this seems like too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like it that much. It dragged on and wasn't that interesting of an idea to start with IMO. Downvoted the article, but I can see the tale fodder, which is a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Work it, work it….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid the crusty edge stuff isn't as clear cut as specific passages: it's a matter of overall feel. This feels bogged down with a lot of terminology that, while there, it doesn't add anything but extra text to the article. it takes my focus away from the person who jumps dimensions and puts it on Protocol This and Operation That. 507's got a simple, memorable image: everyday shmuck who randomly jumps through the multiverse. This is just too busy to get a coherent feel for it. I walk away with a bunch of random terminology in my head, rather than the doomsday research bot.

I think that's my biggest issue. The core thought, the core image, is muddled thoroughly.

To move into something at least partially specific, the priming still bugs me, though I can't put my finger on it. Probably a lot of "you can bloody well jump through universes but you half-ass your way through basic input commands?" Which I suppose is tied to my "Why use a person? A machine is better suited for this" complaint.

So yeah, very subjective. You just managed to find the formula for the hand that pets backwards on the cat of my soul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh really?

…Actually… that might be an interesting spin on the SCP-507 angle. Maybe she can transport people with her, but not back… possibly because of a malfunctioning modification…

…Or maybe she can transport people to & from, but there's some kind of potential downside…

Hmmm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love the idea that she can transport other people with her. But do they come back with her too?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could be wrong

You aren't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really see how to simplify this SCP w/o making it less interesting.

Me neither.

What I'm seeing here is that a lot of people like the idea, references, and certain elements of the execution, and don't mind the rest. But other people trip up on what they see as boring detail, or overall think it could use more.

So… I'm not willing to change the idea, cut the references, or the popular bits of the execution.

But I am willing to push this article harder and add stuff that may be more interesting, if I think it will improve it. I'm also willing to cut some extraneous stuff that isn't core to the idea (or that didn't already earn upvotes, because that's kinda like pulling the rug out from under the people that liked it).

For instance, I just cut out the stuff about being able to transport other people, because it seemed unnecessary and I did nothing with it, it was a 507 trait without the downside attached to the 507 version, and zero people picked it out as something that they loved about the article.

So, keep your thoughts coming. I'm still open to 'em.

(Also, I will be adding an interview log later, but on a separate page in case people like the article but don't like the log.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea could work, but I find it dragged down by all the rest of the extraneous crusty stuff in the article.

Hmmm. Any specific things which stand out as as extraneous?

That, and if this was built by the Foundation, I'm baffled as to why they used a person at all. The machine does the work, just send a probe. It's not like she's stealthy or anything.

Huh. There is a reason. Perhaps I ought to address it. Time to think on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really like it, sorry. It is interesting how a seemingly-harmless SCP can end the world in her alternate dimensions, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I thought this was a knock off of 507.

Then I saw it reference 507. And then it became even crazier.

This is an interesting article. It does a lot of things that are suggested against, but it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Re: Being related to SCP-507, maybe think of this as a movie sequel, building on it, but with enough new things going on that it isn't just a rework of the same idea. I don't really see how to simplify this SCP w/o making it less interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a pretty good article, and then I hit Excursion XJU-034-IQ1. I scrolled up faster than humanly possible and hit upvote like the fist of an angry god. Good show, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not "like 507". It specifically says that 507 appears to have been utilized in the creation of the technology. It was used to research alternate universes with K-class scenarios. It's not ripping off the other article, it does something distinctly different and more controlled. Moose wasn't writing 507 fanfiction here, nor trying to ride on the popularity of the other article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, but it's like SCP-507 except she can return if she dies. I don't know, it's still a bit too similar for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My primary thought was "ain't nobody got time for that".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I could be wrong but there is even a direct redacted reference to that as well. Unless you're thinking of a different project I don't know about.

This is a fairly ambitious SCP. Going to have to let it sit for a bit then come back to read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kept thinking that this was so similar to 507… then I saw the direct crosslink.

I don't know what to think; on the one hand it's decently well written, but on the other hand it reminds me of Kain's mad science experiments (and not necessarily in a good way, either). I can't bring myself to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from the slightly excessive length, and a couple expungements and redactions that I thought were a bit dodgy, I think you've done very well with this. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's still just a really unpleasant article to read. The idea could work, but I find it dragged down by all the rest of the extraneous crusty stuff in the article. I don't feel the cross-references do it much good, mainly in that seeing 507 mentioned reminds me of how much I like that article, which does this one no favors.

That, and if this was built by the Foundation, I'm baffled as to why they used a person at all. The machine does the work, just send a probe. It's not like she's stealthy or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well played Teedles. You earned this upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly. I don't even care that it's long and does a few different things, because that length comes from interesting detail and thoroughness that I like to see in SCPs, and it kept my attention the whole time.

E: Also I love you for including the scorched-earth scenario. And I like the Eris reference, as I did in chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this article is fascinating, and like how it also casually breaks half a dozen common guidelines for SCPs, and does so flawlessly.

Awesome work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love it. Great work all around :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any thoughts on where to cut, or what parts are too weak? (if you have any, of course)

EDIT: I've just done another gigantic pass to fix information presentation, and cut out excess wordage, though I haven't actually changed what's there (except for a minor detail: the altered state's description). Lemme know if that helps, or if (for you) it's just rearranging deck chairs on the Titanic.

EDITx2: To be clear, I changed because I did think it had some readability issues, and I understand it might not actually address your overall problems, although of course I would be pleased if the changes do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I agree, but that doesn't mean I'm right. Any thoughts on further differentiating it? If you've got any, o' course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this honestly doesn't do it for me. This is basically a more intricate version of 507 without the fun bits, such as the log. To clarify, the log for this is fine, but not as good. Yes, it's more likely the Foundation would have something like this than 507, but it's a worse read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
New SCP. My first in a long time. Let's see how it goes over.

A huge thank you to (in no particular order) Wogglebug, Dmatix, Mann, Bright, Echo, Salmander, Djoric, Scantron, Jekeled, Photosynthetic, and everyone else who critiqued this monster, and without whose help this would be much worse than whatever it is now. (Plus also thanks to TroyL, who has critiqued most of my past work and who kept attempting to read this but was simply not able to this time around. The thought really does count sometimes.)

I put a whole lot of effort into this, and I'm willing to put in a whole lot more. Your feedback, give it to me.

P.S. Mackenzie, I added the few tags that I knew were correct. Lemme know if I fucked up it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in chat, too long for too little payoff in my book, and that's enough for a downvote.
